summary: Michael Osborne highlights the critical distinction between an inventory Cryptographic Bill of Materials (CBOM) and a migration CBOM within post-quantum cryptography transition efforts. While an inventory CBOM documents existing cryptography for compliance, a migration CBOM tracks what must change for quantum-safe readiness, including runtime configurations, dependencies, vendor roadmaps, and blockers to activation. This record becomes a vital accountability and planning tool, facilitating coordination not just within organizations but also across vendor and regulatory boundaries. Osborne emphasizes that only a migration CBOM enables automation, gap analysis, and actionable migration strategies. The analogy drawn is to a flight recorder, instrumental for understanding and improving safety—underscoring the migration CBOM’s importance in managing complex, multi-year quantum-safe transformations.
